sentences.chinese: 其使皆私縣官繼物，欲賤市以私其利。
sentences.literal: Their envoys all privately embezzled the state's goods they carried in train, wishing to buy cheap and privatize the profit.
sentences.idiomatic: Envoys skimmed the imperial gifts they escorted, planning to buy cheap on the side and pocket the difference.

sentences.chinese: 外國亦厭漢使人人有言輕重，度漢兵遠，不能至，而禁其食物，以苦漢使。
sentences.literal: The foreign states also wearied of Han envoys, each man having words of weight or lightness; they reckoned Han troops were far and could not arrive, and cut off their food, thereby distressing Han envoys.
sentences.idiomatic: Western courts tired of Han's endless envoys, judged the armies too distant to help, and starved the missions to break them.

sentences.chinese: 漢使乏絕，責怨，至相攻擊。
sentences.literal: Han envoys were exhausted and cut off, blamed and resented one another, and reached the point of attacking one another.
sentences.idiomatic: Stranded missions turned on one another, blaming and even fighting among themselves.

sentences.chinese: 樓蘭、姑師小國，當空道，攻劫漢使王恢等尤甚。
sentences.literal: Loulan and Jushi, small states, stood athwart the open road and attacked and robbed Han envoys such as Wang Hui especially severely.
sentences.idiomatic: The petty kingdoms of Loulan and Jushi straddled the caravan road and preyed worst of all on missions such as Wang Hui's.

sentences.chinese: 而匈奴奇兵又時時遮擊之。
sentences.literal: Moreover Xiongnu irregular troops also from time to time intercepted and struck them.
sentences.idiomatic: Xiongnu raiding parties joined in, picking off stragglers at will.

sentences.chinese: 使者爭言外國利害，皆有城邑，兵弱易擊。
sentences.literal: The envoys vied in speaking of foreign states' profits and harms, that all had walled towns, weak troops, and were easy to strike.
sentences.idiomatic: Returning envoys competed to insist that every western state had cities, feeble armies, and was ripe for the taking.

sentences.chinese: 於是天子遣從票侯破奴將屬國騎及郡兵數萬以擊胡，胡皆去。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven sent Marquis Congpiao Punu leading dependent-state cavalry and commandery troops numbering several tens of thousands to strike the Hu, and the Hu all departed.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor sent Marquis Congpiao Punu at the head of tens of thousands of allied horsemen and local levies; the nomads melted away.

sentences.chinese: 明年，擊破姑師，虜樓蘭王。
sentences.literal: The next year they smashed Jushi and captured the king of Loulan.
sentences.idiomatic: The following year Jushi was crushed and the king of Loulan taken prisoner.

sentences.chinese: 酒泉列亭障至玉門矣。
sentences.literal: From Jiuquan watchtowers and barriers were laid in a line to Jade Gate.
sentences.idiomatic: Signal towers and walls now ran from Jiuquan clear to the Jade Gate.

sentences.chinese: 而大宛諸國發使隨漢使來，觀漢廣大，以大鳥卵及□眩人獻於漢，天子大悅。
sentences.literal: And Dayuan and the various states sent envoys following Han envoys to come, observing Han's vastness, and with large birds' eggs and illusionists (text defective: □) presented them to Han; the Son of Heaven was greatly pleased.
sentences.idiomatic: Dayuan and its neighbors sent retinues back with Han missions to see the empire for themselves, bringing ostrich eggs and conjurers (one character missing in the manuscript); the emperor was delighted.

sentences.chinese: 而漢使窮河源，其山多玉石，採來，天子案古圖書，名河所出山曰崑崙雲。
sentences.literal: And Han envoys traced the river's source to its end; that mountain had much jade and stone; they gathered and brought them; the Son of Heaven examined ancient charts and documents and named the mountain whence the river issues Kunlun, it is said.
sentences.idiomatic: Han scouts tracked the Yellow River to its source, where jade-strewn peaks yielded specimens for the throne; comparing old maps, the court declared those heights Kunlun.

sentences.chinese: 是時，上方數巡狩海上，乃悉從外國客，大都多人則過之，散財帛賞賜，厚具饒給之，以覽視漢富厚焉。
sentences.literal: At this time the sovereign was repeatedly hunting and inspecting along the sea; thereupon he entirely took along foreign guests; where a great metropolis had many people he passed through it, scattering silks and coins as rewards, richly preparing abundant supplies for them, in order to let them survey and see Han's wealth and thickness.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor was touring the coast; he brought foreign guests in his train, detouring through populous cities, showering them with silk and cash and lavish rations so they could see how rich Han was.

sentences.chinese: 大角氐，出奇戲諸怪物，多聚觀者，行賞賜，酒池肉林，令外國客遍觀名各倉庫府臧之積，欲以見漢廣大，傾駭之。
sentences.literal: Great horned wrestlers, displaying strange plays and various monsters, many gathered watchers; he carried out rewards and gifts; pools of wine and forests of meat; he ordered foreign guests everywhere to view the accumulations in each named granary, treasury, and storehouse, wishing thereby to show Han's vastness and overturn and awe them.
sentences.idiomatic: He staged bullfights, exotic spectacles, and freak shows before crowds, heaped rewards on guests, spread the proverbial pools of wine and forests of meat, and marched them through every state granary and treasury—anything to stagger them with Han's scale.

sentences.chinese: 及加其眩者之工，而角氐奇戲歲增變，其益興，自此始。
sentences.literal: And increasing the illusionists' craft, while horned-wrestler strange plays yearly increased and changed, their further flourishing began from this.
sentences.idiomatic: Conjurers grew more skilled, the arena shows multiplied and changed year by year, and the fashion for such display dates from this period.

sentences.chinese: 而外國使更來更去。
sentences.literal: And foreign envoys came and went in alternation.
sentences.idiomatic: Foreign embassies began passing in and out in a steady stream.

sentences.chinese: 大宛以西皆自恃遠，尚驕恣，未可詘以禮羈縻而使也。
sentences.literal: West of Dayuan all relied on distance, still were arrogant and self-willed, and could not be bent by ritual halter-and-bridle and sent as envoys.
sentences.idiomatic: States west of Ferghana trusted their remoteness, stayed arrogant, and could not yet be managed with courtesy alone.

sentences.chinese: 漢使往既多，其少從率進孰於天子，言大宛有善馬在貳師城，匿不肯示漢使。
sentences.literal: Han envoys having gone in great number, their young followers mostly advanced what was ripe to the Son of Heaven, saying Dayuan had fine horses at Ershi city, hid them, and was unwilling to show Han envoys.
sentences.idiomatic: So many missions had returned that junior attendants routinely briefed the throne: Ferghana kept its best bloodstock at Ershi and refused to show Han's envoys.

sentences.chinese: 天子既好宛馬，聞之甘心，使壯士車令等待千金及金馬以請宛王貳師城善馬。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven already loved Ferghana horses; hearing this he was willing at heart, and sent the stalwart Che Ling and others bearing a thousand in gold weight and a golden horse to request of the king of Dayuan the fine horses at Ershi city.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor, already horse-mad, sent Che Ling with a fortune in gold and a golden horse to buy the Ershi studs from the king of Ferghana.

sentences.chinese: 宛國饒漢物，相與謀曰：「漢去我遠，而鹽水中數有敗，出其北有胡寇，出其南乏水草，又且往往而絕邑，乏食者多。
sentences.literal: The state of Dayuan was abundant in Han goods; they plotted together, saying, "Han is far from us, and in the salt water there are frequent disasters; going out northward there are Hu raiders, going out southward there is lack of water and grass, and moreover there are often cut-off settlements; many lack food.
sentences.idiomatic: Ferghana was awash in Han luxuries. Its nobles reasoned: "The capital lies far beyond the salt desert, where caravans founder; north lie the nomads, south little water or grass, and long stretches without towns—most parties starve."

sentences.chinese: 漢使數百人為輩來，常乏食，死者過半，是安能致大軍乎？
sentences.literal: Han envoys come in parties of several hundred men and always lack food; the dead exceed half—how in this case can they bring a great army?
sentences.idiomatic: Han missions arrive in hundreds, usually half dead of hunger—how could they field a real army?

sentences.chinese: 且貳師馬，宛寶馬也。」
sentences.literal: Moreover the Ershi horses are Dayuan's treasure horses."
sentences.idiomatic: Besides, the Ershi horses are our kingdom's treasure."

sentences.chinese: 遂不肯予漢使。
sentences.literal: Thereupon they were unwilling to give them to Han envoys.
sentences.idiomatic: They refused the sale.

sentences.chinese: 漢使怒，妄言，椎金馬而去。
sentences.literal: The Han envoy was angry, spoke wildly, smashed the golden horse, and departed.
sentences.idiomatic: The Han envoy lost his temper, hurled abuse, shattered the golden horse, and stalked off.

sentences.chinese: 宛中貴人怒曰：「漢使至輕我！」
sentences.literal: Dayuan's great men were angry and said, "The Han envoy has come to the utmost in belittling us!"
sentences.idiomatic: Ferghana's grandees cried, "This envoy has insulted us beyond bearing!"

sentences.chinese: 遣漢使去，令其東邊郁成王遮攻，殺漢使，取其財物。
sentences.literal: They sent the Han envoy away, ordered the king of Yucheng on their eastern border to intercept and attack, killed the Han envoy, and took his goods.
sentences.idiomatic: They sent the party on its way, then had Yucheng king on the eastern march ambush it, kill the envoys, and seize the treasure.

sentences.chinese: 天子大怒。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven was greatly angry.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor was furious.

sentences.chinese: 諸嘗使宛姚定漢等言：「宛兵弱，誠以漢兵不過三千人，強弩射之，即破宛矣。」
sentences.literal: Those who had formerly been envoys to Dayuan, Yao Dinghan and others, said, "Dayuan's troops are weak; truly if Han troops do not exceed three thousand men, with strong crossbows shooting them, then Dayuan will be broken at once."
sentences.idiomatic: Veterans of Ferghana such as Yao Dinghan assured the court: "Their army is feeble—three thousand Han crossbowmen would shatter them."

sentences.chinese: 天子以嘗使浞野侯攻樓蘭，以七百騎先至，虜其王，以定漢等言為然，而欲侯寵姬李氏，乃以李廣利為將軍，伐宛。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven because Zhuoye marquis had once attacked Loulan, with seven hundred cavalry arriving first and capturing its king, deemed Yao Dinghan and others' words correct, and wishing to enfeoff the favored concubine Li clan, thereupon made Li Guangli general and attacked Dayuan.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor remembered how Zhuoye marquis had taken Loulan with seven hundred horsemen, believed the optimists, and wanting a fief for his favorite Lady Li's kin, named Li Guangli general and sent him against Ferghana.

sentences.chinese: 孫 猛
sentences.literal: Sun Meng.
sentences.idiomatic: Sun Meng.

sentences.chinese: 騫孫猛，字子游，有俊才，元帝時為光祿大夫，使匈奴，給事中，為石顯所譖。
sentences.literal: Qian's grandson Meng, courtesy name Ziyou, had outstanding talent; in Yuandi's time he was grand counsellor of the palace, was sent to the Xiongnu, was attendant within the palace, and was slandered by Shi Xian.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Qian's grandson Sun Meng, styled Ziyou, was a brilliant man who rose to grand counsellor under Emperor Yuan, served on the Xiongnu embassy staff, sat in the inner court, and fell to Shi Xian's slander.

sentences.chinese: 自殺。
sentences.literal: He killed himself.
sentences.idiomatic: He took his own life.

sentences.chinese: 李廣利
sentences.literal: Li Guangli.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guangli.

sentences.chinese: 李廣利，女弟李夫人有寵於上，產昌邑哀王。
sentences.literal: Li Guangli—his younger sister Lady Li had favor with the sovereign and bore King Ai of Changyi.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Guangli's sister, the favored Lady Li, had borne Prince Ai of Changyi.

sentences.chinese: 太初元年，以廣利為貳師將軍，發屬國六千騎及郡國惡少年數萬人以往，期至貳師城取善馬，故號「貳師將軍」。
sentences.literal: In the first year of Taichu, Guangli was made Ershi general; they raised six thousand cavalry from dependent states and several tens of thousands of rowdy youths from commanderies and kingdoms to go, the term being to reach Ershi city and take fine horses; therefore he was titled "Ershi general."
sentences.idiomatic: In Taichu 1 (104 BCE) he was named Ershi general and given six thousand tribal horse and tens of thousands of adventurers from the commanderies—his brief was to seize the bloodstock at Ershi, whence the title "General of Ershi."

sentences.chinese: 故浩侯王恢使道軍。
sentences.literal: Former Marquis of Hao Wang Hui was sent to guide the army.
sentences.idiomatic: The former Marquis of Hao, Wang Hui, served as route guide for the expedition.

sentences.chinese: 既西過鹽水，當道小國各堅城守，不肯給食，攻之不能下。
sentences.literal: Having gone west and crossed the salt water, each small state athwart the road firmly guarded its walls, was unwilling to supply food, and when attacked could not be taken.
sentences.idiomatic: West of the salt desert every petty state barred its gates, refused provisions, and could not be stormed.

sentences.chinese: 下者得食，不下者數日則去。
sentences.literal: Those taken supplied food; those not taken, after several days, then departed.
sentences.idiomatic: Cities that yielded fed them; those that held out were abandoned after a few days.

sentences.chinese: 比至郁成，士財有數千，皆饑罷。
sentences.literal: By the time they reached Yucheng, soldiers were only several thousand in number, all hungry and exhausted.
sentences.idiomatic: By Yucheng only a few thousand starving, spent men remained.

sentences.chinese: 攻郁成城，郁成距之，所殺傷甚眾。
sentences.literal: They attacked Yucheng city; Yucheng resisted them, and those killed and wounded were very many.
sentences.idiomatic: They assaulted Yucheng; the garrison held, and Han losses were heavy.

sentences.chinese: 貳師將軍與左右計：「至郁成尚不能舉，況至其王都乎？」
sentences.literal: The Ershi general consulted his staff: "Reaching Yucheng we still cannot raise it—how much more their royal capital?"
sentences.idiomatic: General Ershi told his officers, "If we cannot crack Yucheng, what hope have we of the capital?"

sentences.chinese: 引而還。
sentences.literal: He led them and returned.
sentences.idiomatic: He ordered a retreat.

sentences.chinese: 往來二歲，至敦煌，士不過什一二。
sentences.literal: Coming and going two years, reaching Dunhuang, soldiers were not more than one or two in ten.
sentences.idiomatic: Two years out and back brought them to Dunhuang with barely one man in ten.

sentences.chinese: 使使上書言：「道遠，多乏食，且士卒不患戰而患饑。
sentences.literal: He sent envoys to submit a memorial saying, "The road is long, food is often lacking, and moreover the soldiers do not fear battle but fear hunger.
sentences.idiomatic: He memorialized: "The march is long, rations fail, and the men dread hunger more than combat.

sentences.chinese: 人少，不足以拔宛。
sentences.literal: The men are few, insufficient to uproot Dayuan.
sentences.idiomatic: We are too few to take Ferghana.

sentences.chinese: 願且罷兵，益發而復往。」
sentences.literal: We beg for the moment to halt troops, increase levies, and go again."
sentences.idiomatic: Pray let us stand down, raise fresh troops, and try again."

sentences.chinese: 天子聞之，大怒，使使遮玉門關，曰：「軍有敢入，斬之。」
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven heard this and was greatly angry; he sent envoys to block Jade Gate pass, saying, "If any in the army dares enter, behead him."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor raged and posted guards at the Jade Gate: any soldier who crossed inward would die.

sentences.chinese: 貳師恐，因留屯敦煌。
sentences.literal: Ershi was afraid and thereupon remained garrisoned at Dunhuang.
sentences.idiomatic: Ershi dared not advance and camped at Dunhuang.

sentences.chinese: 其夏，漢亡浞野之兵二萬餘於匈奴，公卿議者皆願罷宛軍，專力攻胡。
sentences.literal: That summer Han lost Zhuoye's troops, over twenty thousand men, to the Xiongnu; those who deliberated among the dukes and ministers all wished to halt the Dayuan army and concentrate force on attacking the Hu.
sentences.idiomatic: That summer Han lost twenty thousand men under Zhuoye marquis to the Xiongnu; ministers urged abandoning the Ferghana campaign to hit the nomads alone.

sentences.chinese: 天子業出兵誅宛，宛小國而不能下，則大夏之屬漸輕漢，而宛善馬絕不來，烏孫、輪台易苦漢使，為外國笑。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven having already sent out troops to punish Dayuan—if a small state of Dayuan cannot be taken, then Daxia and the like will gradually despise Han, and Dayuan's fine horses will absolutely not come, Wusun and Luntai will easily distress Han envoys, and it will be laughed at by foreign states.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor had already committed the host: if Han could not crush so small a kingdom, Daxia and the rest would lose respect, Ferghana's horses would never flow east, Wusun and Luntai would bully every envoy, and the western courts would mock the empire.

sentences.chinese: 乃案言伐宛尤不便者鄧光等。
sentences.literal: Thereupon he investigated and prosecuted Deng Guang and others who had said attacking Dayuan was especially inconvenient.
sentences.idiomatic: He had men such as Deng Guang arrested for arguing most loudly against the invasion.

sentences.chinese: 赦囚徒扞寇盜，發惡少年及邊騎，歲余而出敦煌六萬人，負私從者不與。
sentences.literal: He pardoned convicts fit to repel raiders and bandits, raised rowdy youths and frontier cavalry, and after more than a year sixty thousand men marched out of Dunhuang, not counting those who followed on private account.
sentences.idiomatic: Convicts fit for frontier duty, street toughs, and border horse were mobilized; after a year of preparation sixty thousand men crossed from Dunhuang, private followers not counted.

sentences.chinese: 牛十萬，馬三萬匹，驢、橐駝以萬數繼糧，兵弩甚設。
sentences.literal: Cattle one hundred thousand, horses thirty thousand head, donkeys and camels in myriads to carry grain in train, arms and crossbows very fully provided.
sentences.idiomatic: The train included a hundred thousand oxen, thirty thousand horses, and tens of thousands of donkeys and camels for grain, with crossbows and weapons in full supply.

sentences.chinese: 天下騷動，轉相奉伐宛，五十餘校尉。
sentences.literal: All under heaven was stirred; relaying one another they supplied the attack on Dayuan—over fifty colonels.
sentences.idiomatic: The realm seethed as relays of supply fed more than fifty colonels on the Ferghana expedition.

sentences.chinese: 宛城中無井，汲城外流水，於是遣水工徙其城下水空以穴其城。
sentences.literal: Dayuan's city had no wells within; it drew flowing water outside the walls; thereupon he sent water engineers to divert the water below the city hollow so as to tunnel the city.
sentences.idiomatic: Ferghana's capital had no wells and drew water from a stream outside; Han sent engineers to divert the flow underground and undermine the walls.

sentences.chinese: 益發戍甲卒十八萬酒泉、張掖北，置居延、休屠以衛酒泉。
sentences.literal: He further raised garrison armored soldiers one hundred eighty thousand north of Jiuquan and Zhangye, established Juyan and Xiutu to guard Jiuquan.
sentences.idiomatic: Another hundred eighty thousand armored men were posted north of Jiuquan and Zhangye, with Juyan and Xiutu garrisons shielding Jiuquan.

sentences.chinese: 而發天下七科適，及載□給貳師，轉車人徒相連屬至敦煌。
sentences.literal: And he raised the empire's seven categories of transport levy, and loaded [lacuna: □] to supply Ershi; transport carts and men followed one another in succession to Dunhuang.
sentences.idiomatic: The seven classes liable for corvée were called up, convoys (one commodity name missing in the text) were loaded for Ershi, and wagon trains of laborers stretched all the way to Dunhuang.

sentences.chinese: 而拜習馬者二人為執驅馬校尉，備破宛擇取其善馬雲。
sentences.literal: And he appointed two men skilled with horses as colonels of the led horse, preparing for when Dayuan was broken to select and take its fine horses, it is said.
sentences.idiomatic: Two expert horsemen were named colonels of the led horse, ready to pick the best stock once Ferghana fell.

sentences.chinese: 於是貳師後復行，兵多，所至小國莫不迎，出食給軍。
sentences.literal: Thereupon Ershi afterward again marched; the troops were many; wherever they arrived the small states without exception welcomed them and brought out food to supply the army.
sentences.idiomatic: When Ershi marched again, his host was so large that every petty state along the way opened its granaries.

sentences.chinese: 至輪台，輪台不下，攻數日，屠之。
sentences.literal: Reaching Luntai, Luntai would not submit; they attacked several days and slaughtered it.
sentences.idiomatic: At Luntai, which refused to yield, they stormed for days and put the town to the sword.

sentences.chinese: 自此而西，平行至宛城，兵到者三萬。
sentences.literal: From this westward, going level they reached Dayuan city; troops that arrived were thirty thousand.
sentences.idiomatic: West of that point they marched level ground to the Ferghana capital with thirty thousand effectives.

sentences.chinese: 宛兵迎擊漢兵，漢兵射敗之，宛兵走入保其城。
sentences.literal: Dayuan troops met and struck Han troops; Han troops shot and defeated them; Dayuan troops fled in and defended their city.
sentences.idiomatic: Ferghana sortied; Han archery broke them, and they bolted behind the walls.

sentences.chinese: 貳師欲攻郁成城，恐留行而令宛益生詐，乃先至宛，決其水原，移之，則宛固已憂困。
sentences.literal: Ershi wished to attack Yucheng city, but feared delaying the march and making Dayuan increase deceit the more; thereupon he first reached Dayuan, cut its water source, and diverted it, so that Dayuan was already anxious and distressed.
sentences.idiomatic: Ershi thought of striking Yucheng first but feared delay would let Ferghana grow bolder; he drove straight to the capital, cut the aqueduct, and left the city parched and desperate.

sentences.chinese: 圍其城，攻之四十餘日。
sentences.literal: They surrounded its city and attacked it over forty days.
sentences.idiomatic: They invested the walls for more than forty days.

sentences.chinese: 其外城壞，虜宛貴人勇將煎靡。
sentences.literal: Its outer wall broke; they captured Dayuan's great man, the brave general Jianmi.
sentences.idiomatic: The outer ring fell; Han took the noble general Jianmi prisoner.

sentences.chinese: 宛大恐，走入中城，相與謀曰：「漢所為攻宛，以王毋寡。」
sentences.literal: Dayuan was greatly afraid, fled into the inner city, and plotted together, saying, "What Han does in attacking Dayuan is because of King Wugua."
sentences.idiomatic: Panic sent them into the inner citadel, where nobles muttered, "Han is here for King Wugua."

sentences.chinese: 宛貴人謀曰：「王毋寡匿善馬，殺漢使。
sentences.literal: Dayuan's great men plotted, saying, "King Wugua hid fine horses and killed Han envoys.
sentences.idiomatic: The grandees said, "Wugua hid the horses and murdered the envoys.

sentences.chinese: 今殺王而出善馬，漢兵宜解；
sentences.literal: Now if we kill the king and bring out fine horses, Han troops ought to disperse;
sentences.idiomatic: Kill him, hand over the studs, and Han should lift the siege;

sentences.chinese: 即不，乃力戰而死，未晚也。」
sentences.literal: if not, then we fight with full force and die—it is not yet late."
sentences.idiomatic: if not, we fight to the death—there is still time."

sentences.chinese: 宛貴人皆以為然，共殺王。
sentences.literal: Dayuan's great men all deemed it right and together killed the king.
sentences.idiomatic: They agreed and slew the king together.

sentences.chinese: 持其頭，遣人使貳師，約曰：「漢無攻我，我盡出善馬，恣所取，而給漢軍食。
sentences.literal: They held his head, sent men to Ershi, and covenanted, saying, "If Han does not attack us, we will bring out all fine horses, let you take as you will, and moreover supply Han army food.
sentences.idiomatic: They sent his head to Ershi with terms: "Spare us, we will parade every good horse for your choice and feed your army."

sentences.chinese: 即不聽我，我盡殺善馬，康居之救又且至。
sentences.literal: If you do not heed us, we will kill all fine horses, and moreover Kangju's relief will soon arrive.
sentences.idiomatic: Refuse, and we slaughter the bloodstock; Kangju's relief is already on the way."

sentences.chinese: 至，我居內，康居居外，與漢軍戰。
sentences.literal: When they arrive, we dwell within, Kangju dwells without, and we fight Han troops.
sentences.idiomatic: When they come, we hold the inner city while Kangju strikes from without."

sentences.chinese: 孰計之，何從？」
sentences.literal: Weigh it carefully—which course to follow?"
sentences.idiomatic: Weigh our choices—which way do you take?"

sentences.chinese: 是時，康居候視漢兵尚盛，不敢進。
sentences.literal: At this time Kangju spied and saw Han troops were still strong and did not dare advance.
sentences.idiomatic: Kangju scouts saw how strong Han still was and hung back.

sentences.chinese: 貳師聞宛城中新得漢人知穿井，而其內食尚多。
sentences.literal: Ershi heard that within Dayuan city newly obtained Han men knew how to dig wells, and moreover food within was still abundant.
sentences.idiomatic: Ershi learned that Han prisoners inside were sinking wells and that the citadel still held ample grain.

sentences.chinese: 計以為來誅首惡者毋寡，毋寡頭已至，如此不許，則堅守，而康居候漢兵罷來救宛，破漢軍必矣。
sentences.literal: He reckoned that they had come to execute the chief evil-doer Wugua; Wugua's head had already arrived; if in this case he did not consent, then they would firmly hold the defense, while Kangju waited for Han troops to tire and come save Dayuan—breaking Han troops was certain.
sentences.idiomatic: Han had marched to punish Wugua; with that head delivered, to refuse the offer meant a prolonged siege while Kangju waited for Han exhaustion—and Han would surely be broken.

sentences.chinese: 軍吏皆以為然，許宛之約。
sentences.literal: The army officers all deemed it right and granted Dayuan's covenant.
sentences.idiomatic: His officers agreed, and he accepted Ferghana's offer.

sentences.chinese: 宛乃出其馬，令漢自擇之，而多出食食漢軍。
sentences.literal: Dayuan thereupon brought out its horses, ordered Han to choose them itself, and moreover brought out much food to feed Han troops.
sentences.idiomatic: Ferghana led out its horses for Han to pick and piled up rations for the army.

sentences.chinese: 漢軍取其善馬數十匹，中馬以下牝牡三千餘匹，而立宛貴人之故時遇漢善者名昧蔡為宛王，與盟而罷兵，終不得入中城，罷而引歸。
sentences.literal: Han troops took their fine horses several tens of head, and below middle-grade stallions and mares over three thousand head, and established the Dayuan great man who in the past had treated Han well, named Mocai, as king of Dayuan, swore covenant with him and halted troops; in the end they could not enter the inner city, halted and led the army back.
sentences.idiomatic: Han took several dozen top mounts and more than three thousand lesser animals, enthroned the pro-Han noble Mocai, swore peace, and withdrew without ever entering the inner citadel.

sentences.chinese: 初，貳師起孰煌西，為人多，道上國不能食，分為數軍，從南北道。
sentences.literal: At first, when Ershi rose west of Shu-huang (text: 孰煌, read Dunhuang), because the men were many the states along the road could not feed them; he divided into several armies, following south and north routes.
sentences.idiomatic: When Ershi first marched west from Dunhuang (the manuscript writes 孰煌 for 敦煌), his host was too large for the oases to feed, so he split into several columns on northern and southern tracks.

sentences.chinese: 校尉王申生、故鴻臚壺充國等千餘人別至郁成，城守不肯給食。
sentences.literal: Colonel Wang Shensheng, former grand herald Hu Chongguo, and over a thousand others separately reached Yucheng; it guarded the walls and was unwilling to supply food.
sentences.idiomatic: Colonel Wang Shensheng and the former grand herald Hu Chongguo led a thousand men to Yucheng, which barred its gates and refused provisions.

sentences.chinese: 申生去大軍二百里，負而輕之，攻郁成急。
sentences.literal: Shensheng was two hundred li from the great army, relied on this and treated them lightly, and attacked Yucheng urgently.
sentences.idiomatic: Two hundred li from the main army, Shensheng grew reckless and pressed the assault on Yucheng.

sentences.chinese: 郁成窺知申生軍少，晨用三千人攻殺申生等，數人脫亡，走貳師。
sentences.literal: Yucheng spied and knew Shensheng's troops were few; at dawn it used three thousand men to attack and kill Shensheng and the rest; several men escaped and fled to Ershi.
sentences.idiomatic: Yucheng saw how small his force was and at dawn sent three thousand men to wipe it out; only a handful escaped to Ershi.

sentences.chinese: 貳師令搜粟都尉上官桀往攻破郁成，郁成降。
sentences.literal: Ershi ordered the search-grain commandant Shangguan Jie to go and break Yucheng; Yucheng submitted.
sentences.idiomatic: Ershi sent Search-Grain commandant Shangguan Jie, who stormed Yucheng into submission.

sentences.chinese: 其王亡走康居，桀追至康居。
sentences.literal: Its king fled and ran to Kangju; Jie pursued to Kangju.
sentences.idiomatic: Its king fled to Kangju, and Jie followed him there.

sentences.chinese: 康居聞漢已破宛，出郁成王與桀，桀令四騎士縛守詣大將軍。
sentences.literal: Kangju heard Han had already broken Dayuan, brought out the king of Yucheng and gave him to Jie; Jie ordered four mounted knights to bind and guard him and convey him to the grand general.
sentences.idiomatic: Learning that Han had taken Ferghana, Kangju handed over the king of Yucheng; Jie assigned four horsemen to bind him and escort him to the grand general.

sentences.chinese: 四人相謂「郁成，漢所毒，今生將，卒失大事。」
sentences.literal: The four said to one another, "Yucheng is what Han hates; now alive as a captive, in the end we lose the great affair."
sentences.idiomatic: The four muttered, "Yucheng is Han's bitter enemy; delivering him alive will ruin everything."

sentences.chinese: 欲殺，莫適先擊。
sentences.literal: They wished to kill him, but none was appropriate to strike first.
sentences.idiomatic: They meant to kill him, yet none would strike the first blow.

sentences.chinese: 上□騎士趙弟拔劍擊斬郁成王。
sentences.literal: The [lacuna] mounted knight Zhao Di drew his sword, struck, and beheaded the king of Yucheng.
sentences.idiomatic: The cavalryman Zhao Di (one character missing before his title in the text) drew steel and cut down the king of Yucheng.

sentences.chinese: 桀等遂追及大將軍。
sentences.literal: Jie and the rest thereupon caught up with the grand general.
sentences.idiomatic: Jie and his party then rejoined the grand general's column.

sentences.chinese: 初，貳師後行，天子使使告烏孫大發兵擊宛。
sentences.literal: At first, when Ershi marched later, the Son of Heaven sent envoys to inform Wusun to raise a great army and strike Dayuan.
sentences.idiomatic: During Ershi's second march the emperor sent word to Wusun to mobilize heavily against Ferghana.

sentences.chinese: 烏孫發二千騎往，持兩端，不肯前。
sentences.literal: Wusun raised two thousand cavalry to go, held both ends, and was unwilling to advance.
sentences.idiomatic: Wusun sent two thousand horse but sat on the fence and would not close with the enemy.

sentences.chinese: 貳師將軍之東，諸所過小國聞宛破，皆使其子弟從入貢獻，見天子，因為質焉。
sentences.literal: When the Ershi general went east, the various small states he passed heard Dayuan was broken and all sent their sons and younger brothers to follow in and present tribute, see the Son of Heaven, and thereby become hostages.
sentences.idiomatic: On Ershi's return march every petty state along the road, hearing Ferghana had fallen, sent princes in his train to court with tribute and stayed as hostages.

sentences.chinese: 軍還，入玉門者萬餘人，馬千餘匹。
sentences.literal: The army returned; those who entered Jade Gate were over ten thousand men, horses over a thousand head.
sentences.idiomatic: More than ten thousand men and a thousand horses crossed back through the Jade Gate.

sentences.chinese: 後行，非乏食，戰死不甚多，而將吏貪，不愛卒，侵牟之，以此物故者眾。
sentences.literal: On the later march it was not lack of food; battle deaths were not very many, but generals and officers were greedy, did not cherish the soldiers, and encroached on them; for this reason casualties from such causes were many.
sentences.idiomatic: The second expedition did not starve and lost few in battle, yet greedy officers preyed on the ranks, and most deaths came from that abuse.

sentences.chinese: 天子為萬里征伐，不錄其過，乃下詔曰：「匈奴為害久矣，今雖徙幕北，與旁國謀共要絕大月氏使，遮殺中郎將江、故雁門守攘。
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven because of the ten-thousand-li campaign and conquest did not record their faults; thereupon he issued an edict saying, "The Xiongnu have been a harm long; now although they have moved north of the desert curtain, they plot with neighboring states jointly to intercept and cut off Greater Yuezhi envoys, intercepting and killing palace gentleman Jiang and former Yanmen guardian Rang.
sentences.idiomatic: For a march of ten thousand li the emperor overlooked their faults and proclaimed: "The Xiongnu have long plagued the border; though driven north of the desert, they still league with neighbors to ambush Greater Yuezhi embassies and have cut down Palace Gentleman Jiang and former Yanmen warden Rang."

sentences.chinese: 危須以西及大宛皆合約殺期門車令、中郎將朝及身毒國使，隔東西道。
sentences.literal: West of Yanxu and including Dayuan all jointly in covenant killed Expectant Gates Che Ling, palace gentleman Chao, and the Shendu-state envoy, cutting off east-west roads.
sentences.idiomatic: From Yanxu west to Ferghana they conspired to murder Expectant Gates Che Ling, Palace Gentleman Chao, and the Indian envoy, severing the road between east and west.

sentences.chinese: 貳師將軍廣利征討厥罪，伐勝大宛。
sentences.literal: Ershi general Guangli campaigned and punished their crimes, attacked and overcame Dayuan.
sentences.idiomatic: General Ershi Li Guangli marched to punish those crimes and conquered Ferghana.

sentences.chinese: 賴天之靈，從溯河山，涉流沙，通西海，山雪不積，士大夫徑度，獲王首虜，珍怪之物畢陳於闕。
sentences.literal: Relying on heaven's numinous power, following upstream rivers and mountains, crossing shifting sands, opening to the western sea, mountain snow not accumulating, gentlemen officers went straight across, captured kings' heads and captives, and strange precious things were all displayed at the palace gate.
sentences.idiomatic: By heaven's favor they traced rivers, crossed the Gobi, reached the western sea, marched snow-free heights, took enemy kings captive, and heaped rare tribute before the palace.

sentences.chinese: 其封廣利為海西侯，食邑八千戶。」
sentences.literal: Let Guangli be enfeoffed marquis of Haixi, with a fief of eight thousand households."
sentences.idiomatic: Let Li Guangli be enfeoffed as Marquis of Haixi with eight thousand households.
